input.prompt:
I'm building a chat messaging screen in .NET MAUI targeting .NET 10. When the
keyboard opens, it covers my text input at the bottom of the screen. In .NET 9
I used WindowSoftInputModeAdjust.Resize but that doesn't seem to work anymore.
How do I make the input bar move up when the keyboard appears?

input.initialPrompt:
I'm building a chat messaging screen in .NET MAUI targeting .NET 10. When the
keyboard opens, it covers my text input at the bottom of the screen. In .NET 9
I used WindowSoftInputModeAdjust.Resize but that doesn't seem to work anymore.
How do I make the input bar move up when the keyboard appears?

data.reasoningText:
The user is asking about a .NET MAUI issue where the keyboard covers the text input at the bottom of the screen. They mention that `WindowSoftInputModeAdjust.Resize` worked in .NET 9 but doesn't work in .NET 10.

This is a known change in .NET MAUI. In .NET 10 (which targets Android 15+ / API 35), edge-to-edge display is enforced by default, and the old `WindowSoftInputModeAdjust.Resize` approach was deprecated in Android API 30 and no longer works.

The solution in .NET 10 / Android 15+ is to use the new `KeyboardAcceleratorsEnabled` or more specifically, to handle insets properly. 

Since `WindowSoftInputModeAdjust.Resize` was already deprecated in Android 11, the proper fix for .NET 10 is to listen for keyboard insets using `WindowInsetsCompat` and adjust the view's padding dynamically when the IME appears or disappears. For .NET MAUI in .NET 10, the practical approach is to use a `Grid` layout with the input anchored at the bottom, then handle keyboard insets through platform-specific code that detects when the IME appears and adjusts the view accordingly.

Writing platform-specific code... I'm realizing there's a simpler path for .NET 10 MAUI—the framework now has better built-in keyboard handling, though the older `KeyboardAutoManagerScroll` feature from .NET 8 that auto-scrolls focused elements might not work well for chat UI patterns where you need the input bar to push up from the bottom. For this specific use case, I should focus on using the Android WindowInsets API to detect keyboard height and adjust the bottom padding accordingly.
